title:
4927. Design a CheerCoin Distributed Reward System
Hard

Design a high-level architecture for 'CheerCoin', a virtual cur

transcript.text:
4927. Design a CheerCoin Distributed Reward System
Hard

Design a high-level architecture for 'CheerCoin', a virtual currency system used for rewarding and gifting within a large-scale platform.

Functional Requirements:
Users must be able to earn CheerCoins through specific platform activities.
Users can 'cheer' for others, which involves transferring coins from their balance to another user.
The system must provide a real-time view of a user's current coin balance.
Non-Functional Requirements:
Scalability: The system must handle millions of concurrent users and potential viral peaks in cheering activity.
Consistency: Transactions must be atomic, ensuring that a user's balance is always accurate and durable.
Latency: The earning and spending process should feel instantaneous to the end-user.
Provide the architecture, data models, and a discussion on how to maintain consistency in a distributed environment.

blocks.md:
# CheerCoin — Distributed Reward & Wallet System

Money's involved, so the whole game here is: **make balances feel instant while never losing or double-counting a single coin.** Let me build that up the way I'd do it live — requirements and structure first, then I'll earn every piece of complexity.

> 🎙️ "Before I draw anything, let me frame the problem and lock requirements, because for a currency system the requirements *are* the design — the hard part is consistency, not boxes."

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one flow at a time — earn, cheer, view balance — then go deep on the two or three hardest parts: atomic transfers, exactly-once earning, and hot-key viral cheering. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: **a wallet wants strong, atomic, durable consistency (it's money), but "instantaneous" + "millions concurrent + viral peaks" pushes toward caching and async — which fight consistency.** Resolving that tension *is* the interview.

🏢 **Company/level lens:** I'll target an **Uber/payments-style bar at SDE-2/senior level** — real money semantics, a clear transaction state machine, hot-shard ("viral creator") thinking, and operational rigor (idempotency, reconciliation, on-call). Tell me if you'd rather I tilt Google (go deeper on consensus) or startup (simplest shippable), and I'll re-tilt.

**Functional — core flows (above the line):**
- **Earn:** users earn CheerCoins from platform activities (e.g. completing an action, getting cheered, daily bonus) — credited to their balance.
- **Cheer (transfer):** a user moves coins from their own balance to another user's balance — atomic debit-one, credit-other.
- **View balance:** a user sees their current balance in real time.

**Below the line (out of scope today):** buying coins with real money / payment rails, cash-out/withdrawal to fiat, leaderboards & gift catalogs, fraud-ML scoring (I'll note hooks), admin clawbacks, tax reporting. I'll mention where each plugs in.

**Non-Functional — talkable targets:**
- **Consistency:** transfers and earns are **atomic and durable** — a balance is never negative, coins are never created or destroyed by the app. Balance-affecting ops are **strongly consistent**; the *displayed* balance after your own action must reflect it (read-your-own-writes). Strong > available when they conflict for the ledger.
- **Availability:** target **99.99%** ("four nines", ~52 min/yr) for balance reads; writes (cheer/earn) target **99.9%** — I'll favor *refusing* a transfer over double-spending it.
- **Latency:** balance view **p99 < 100ms**; cheer/earn **p99 < 300ms** end-to-end so it feels instant.
- **Scale:** millions concurrent; design for **~100M DAU**, and absorb **viral peaks** where one popular creator receives a huge fan-out of cheers (hot key).
- **Durability:** zero coin loss — **RPO ≈ 0** for the ledger (no committed transaction lost).

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true *zero* coin loss with *zero* latency is impossible across regions — I'll pin earning to a single write-region per user and accept bounded cross-region read staleness; I'll call the exact bound in §8 and the ledger.

> 💬 "The thing I most want to get right is that this is a closed-loop currency — every coin that leaves one balance must land in exactly one other balance, even under retries and crashes. So I'll treat it like a payments ledger, not a counter."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "There are really three things users do: earn coins, cheer someone by transferring coins, and check their balance. The non-functional side is what makes it hard — it's money, so every transaction has to be atomic and durable, a balance can never go negative, and yet it has to feel instant for a hundred million daily users with viral spikes. When consistency and availability collide on the ledger, I'll choose consistency — I'd rather decline a cheer than let someone spend coins they don't have."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each one moves the design:

- **Can a balance go negative / is overdraft ever allowed?** → If never, every debit needs a conditional check; this drives the single-writer-per-account design. *(Assume: never negative.)*
- **Is CheerCoin purchasable with real money or purely earned?** → If real money enters, I need payment idempotency + audit/compliance (SOX). *(Assume: earned-only today, but I'll build a real ledger so adding purchases later is trivial.)*
- **Is there a global "treasury"/mint for earned coins, or are coins conjured per earn?** → Affects whether earn is a transfer-from-treasury (true conservation) or a credit. *(Assume: a system treasury account mints earns — keeps the books balanced: every credit has a matching debit.)*
- **Do we need full transaction history per user, or just balance?** → Drives whether the ledger is the source of truth or just balance rows. *(Assume: yes, immutable history — needed for audit & dispute.)*
- **Strict global ordering of a user's transactions, or just per-account correctness?** → *(Assume: per-account serializability is enough; no global clock needed.)*
- **Expected viral skew** — how hot can one receiver get? → Sizes the hot-key solution. *(Assume: a top creator could receive 10K+ cheers/sec in a spike.)*
- **Regulatory/cash-out?** → *(Assume: no fiat cash-out yet, so it's "play money" legally, but I'll still keep an auditable ledger.)*

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:** ~500M registered / ~100M DAU; coins are integers (no fractional cents — avoids float entirely); single home region per user with async global replication; a treasury/mint account exists; earns are idempotent on an event id; cheers are idempotent on a client-supplied key.

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — most important one is *never negative* and *earned-only for now*. Want to challenge any before I size it and start drawing?"

---

Now let me put rough numbers on it — I'll compute only what changes a decision.

blocks.name: run_command

input.command:
cat > capacity.py << 'EOF'
DAU = 100_000_000
SEC = 86_400

# --- Reads: balance views (hot path) ---
views_per_user_day = 10
balance_reads_day = DAU * views_per_user_day
read_qps = balance_reads_day / SEC
read_peak = read_qps * 5

# --- Writes: earns + cheers (ledger appends) ---
earns_per_user_day = 5
cheers_per_user_day = 2
writes_day = DAU * (earns_per_user_day + cheers_per_user_day)
write_qps = writes_day / SEC
write_peak_5x = write_qps * 5
write_peak_viral = write_qps * 10

ratio = balance_reads_day / writes_day

# --- Ledger storage ---
# entry: id(16)+from(8)+to(8)+amount(8)+type(2)+idem_key(16)+ts(8)+status(2)+meta(~50)
row_bytes = 16+8+8+8+2+16+8+2+50           # ~118 -> call it 150 w/ overhead
row_bytes = 150
raw_day = writes_day * row_bytes
# indexes (~2x) + replication (3x) + compaction/WAL headroom (~1.5x)
eff_mult = 2 * 3 * 1.5
stored_day = raw_day * eff_mult
stored_year = stored_day * 365

# --- Redis balance cache sizing ---
# key: user_id->balance, ~ 64 bytes/entry incl overhead, for all DAU hot
cache_entry = 64
cache_bytes = DAU * cache_entry

# --- single hot account ---
hot_qps = 10_000  # viral receiver

def h(n):
    for u,d in [("T",1e12),("B",1e9),("M",1e6),("K",1e3)]:
        if abs(n)>=d: return f"{n/d:.1f}{u}"
    return f"{n:.0f}"

print("READS")
print("  balance reads/day :", h(balance_reads_day))
print("  read QPS avg      :", h(read_qps))
print("  read QPS peak(5x) :", h(read_peak))
print("WRITES")
print("  ledger writes/day :", h(writes_day))
print("  write QPS avg     :", h(write_qps))
print("  write peak 5x     :", h(write_peak_5x))
print("  write peak viral  :", h(write_peak_viral))
print("RATIO read:write    :", round(ratio,0),":1")
print("STORAGE")
print("  raw/day           :", h(raw_day),"B")
print("  effective/day     :", h(stored_day),"B  (x%.0f for idx+3x repl+headroom)"%eff_mult)
print("  effective/year    :", h(stored_year),"B")
print("CACHE")
print("  all-DAU balances  :", h(cache_bytes),"B")
print("HOT ACCOUNT qps     :", h(hot_qps))
EOF
python3 capacity.py

blocks.output:
READS
  balance reads/day : 1.0B
  read QPS avg      : 11.6K
  read QPS peak(5x) : 57.9K
WRITES
  ledger writes/day : 700.0M
  write QPS avg     : 8.1K
  write peak 5x     : 40.5K
  write peak viral  : 81.0K
RATIO read:write    : 1.0 :1
STORAGE
  raw/day           : 105.0B B
  effective/day     : 945.0B B  (x9 for idx+3x repl+headroom)
  effective/year    : 344.9T B
CACHE
  all-DAU balances  : 6.4B B
HOT ACCOUNT qps     : 10.0K

blocks.md:
## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) I'm deferring full estimation — I only computed the few numbers that actually steer the design. Quick glossary: **QPS** = queries (requests) per second; **TTL** = time-to-live, an auto-expiry on a cached entry. | Metric | Value | Talkable | |---|---|---| | Balance reads/day | 1.0B | "a billion balance checks a day" | | Read QPS (avg → peak) | 11.6K → ~58K | "tens of thousands of reads/sec" | | Ledger writes/day | 700M | "earns + cheers, ~700M/day" | | Write QPS (avg → peak) | 8.1K → ~40K, viral ~80K | "up to ~80K writes/sec in a spike" | | Read : write ratio | ~1 : 1 | "unusually write-heavy for a 'read' system" | | Ledger storage/yr | ~345 TB (idx + 3x replica + headroom) | "low hundreds of TB/yr" | | All-DAU balance cache | ~6.4 GB | "fits in RAM easily" | | Hot (viral) account | ~10K cheers/sec to ONE row | **the number that forces the design** | **The one number that forces a choice:** a single viral receiver taking **~10K writes/sec to one balance row**. A normal Postgres row handles maybe a few hundred concurrent updates/sec before lock contention; one row at 10K/s will serialize and melt. *That* single fact forces per-account sharding + hot-key handling (sharded counters / async aggregation), which I'll deep-dive in §8. > 🎙️ **Script:** "The headline isn't the totals — it's that this is roughly one-to-one reads to writes, which is write-heavy for something called a 'view balance' system, so I can't just cache my way out. And the real killer is concentration: in a viral moment, ten thousand cheers a second can hit one person's balance — a single database row can't take that, so per-account sharding and hot-key handling are going to drive my whole design. Storage is a non-issue, a few hundred terabytes a year, and all the balances fit in a few gigs of RAM." --- ## 4. Core Entities First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model (§7); keeping it plain for now: - **User / Account** — holds a `balance` and is the owner of coins; the thing we debit and credit. - **Treasury (System Account)** — the mint; earns are transfers *from* treasury so the books always balance (sum of all balances is conserved). - **Transaction (Ledger Entry)** — the immutable, append-only record of one money movement: from, to, amount, type (earn/cheer), status, idempotency key, timestamp. **This is the source of truth.** - **Balance** — a fast, derived projection of an account's current coins (a materialized running total) for instant reads. - **Earn Event** — the platform activity that triggers an earn (used as the idempotency key so we never double-credit). - **Idempotency Key** — a dedup token attached to a cheer/earn so retries don't double-apply. > 🎙️ **Script:** "The key entities are accounts with balances, an immutable transaction ledger that's the real source of truth, a treasury account that mints earned coins so the books always balance, and idempotency keys to make retries safe. The balance itself I treat as a derived value — a fast running total I can always rebuild from the ledger. I'll flesh out the exact fields in the data model once the API's clear." --- ## 5. API / Interface REST/JSON over HTTPS. The caller is **always identified from their session/JWT** — I never trust a client-supplied `from_user`, amount source, or timestamp. (**JWT** = a signed token proving who's calling.) **Earn** (serves "earn coins") — usually server-initiated, but as an endpoint: ``` POST /v1/earn Header: Idempotency-Key: Body: { "amount": 50, "reason": "daily_bonus", "event_id": "evt_abc" } Resp: 201 { "txn_id": "...", "new_balance": 1050 } ``` *Why:* POST creates a ledger entry. The earn event id is the idempotency key — replaying the same event credits **once**. Amount/reason are validated server-side against the activity, never trusted raw. **Cheer** (serves "transfer coins") — the heart of the system: ``` POST /v1/cheer Header: Idempotency-Key: Body: { "to_user": "u_456", "amount": 10, "message": "nice work!" } Resp: 201 { "txn_id": "...", "new_balance": 990 } 409 { "error": "insufficient_funds" } 200 { "txn_id": "...", ... } // idempotent replay of a prior success ``` *Why:* POST creates a transfer. `from_user` = the authenticated caller (not in body). Idempotency-Key makes a retried network call safe — a replay returns the **same** original result (200), never a second debit. 409 on insufficient funds — we **refuse**, never overdraw. **View balance** (serves "real-time balance"): ``` GET /v1/balance -> 200 { "balance": 990, "as_of": "..." } GET /v1/transactions?cursor=... -> paginated history ``` *Why:* GET, idempotent/cacheable. `as_of` lets the client reason about staleness. History is cursor-paginated (no offset scans). > 💬 "Notice the cheer carries an Idempotency-Key and the sender is taken from the token, not the body — that's the whole anti-double-spend and anti-spoofing story in the API itself." > 🎙️ **Script:** "Three endpoints map one-to-one to the requirements: POST earn, POST cheer, GET balance. The two writes both carry an idempotency key so a retry never double-applies — for earn it's the event id, for cheer it's a client uuid. The sender always comes from the auth token, never the request body, so you can't cheer from someone else's wallet. And on insufficient funds I return a clean 409 — I decline rather than go negative." --- ## 6. High-Level Design (one requirement at a time) I'll start minimal and wire each flow onto the last. Palette: green = my services (hot path), amber = async/queues, slate = datastores, blue = clients, purple = external. ### 6.1 "Users can earn CheerCoins" This slice: an activity happens, we durably append an earn to the ledger and update the balance — exactly once. | Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | Wallet Service | Stateless Go/Java svc | Owns all balance mutations behind one door | Another service to run | | Ledger + Balance DB | PostgreSQL, sharded by account_id | ACID — debit/credit must be one atomic txn; money needs strong consistency | Sharding/ops complexity | | Idempotency store | Same Postgres txn (unique index on idem_key) | Dedup earns in the *same* transaction → truly exactly-once | Index write cost | > 🎙️ **Script (choices):** "I'm putting all money mutations behind a single Wallet Service so there's exactly one code path that can move coins — that's how I keep the invariants in one place. I'm using Postgres because a debit and credit have to commit together atomically — ACID is non-negotiable for money. And I make earn exactly-once by putting a unique index on the idempotency key *inside the same transaction* as the credit, so a replay hits the unique-violation and we return the original result instead of crediting twice." ```mermaid flowchart LR Act[Activity event]:::ext -->|1 earn evt| WS[Wallet Service]:::svc WS -->|2 txn: insert ledger + bump balance| DB[(Ledger + Balance DB
Postgres sharded)]:::store WS -->|3 new_balance| Act classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe; ``` ↳ reuses existing: none yet (this is the foundation). 1. An **activity event** (or the POST /earn) hits the **Wallet Service** with the event id as idempotency key. 2. In **one Postgres transaction**: insert ledger row `treasury → user, amount` (unique on idem_key) **and** `UPDATE balance SET balance = balance + amount`. **State change:** treasury debited, user credited, both committed or neither. 3. Return the new balance. A replayed event hits the unique index → we read back and return the original — **no double credit**. > 💬 "Earn is just a transfer from the treasury, so the books stay balanced — coins aren't conjured, they're moved from the mint." ### 6.2 "Users can cheer (transfer coins to another user)" This slice: atomically debit sender, credit receiver, refuse if insufficient — and survive retries. New piece: an async fan-out for notifications/feed so we don't block the cheer. | Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | Cheer/Transfer logic | In Wallet Service | Reuse the single money door | — | | Notification stream | Kafka | Durable buffer so notifying the receiver never blocks or fails the money txn | Adds a moving part | | Notifier/Feed svc | Consumer | Push "you got cheered" async | Eventual (fine) | > 🎙️ **Script (choices):** "The transfer itself stays in the Wallet Service and in one Postgres transaction — debit the sender with a guard that the balance stays non-negative, credit the receiver, write one ledger entry, all atomic. The *notification* I push onto Kafka — a durable log — so telling the receiver 'you got cheered' happens asynchronously and can never roll back or slow down the actual money movement. If the notifier is down, the coins still moved and the event waits in the log." ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Sender app]:::client -->|1 POST cheer + idem key| GW[API Gateway]:::svc GW -->|2| WS[Wallet Service]:::svc WS -->|3 atomic debit+credit+ledger| DB[(Ledger + Balance DB
Postgres sharded)]:::store WS -->|4 emit cheer event| K[[Kafka cheer-events]]:::async K -->|5 async| NF[Notifier / Feed svc]:::svc WS -->|6 new_balance| S classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe; ``` ↳ reuses existing: Wallet Service + Ledger/Balance DB (from §6.1). 1. Sender app POSTs the cheer with an **Idempotency-Key**. 2. **API Gateway** authenticates (sender = token), rate-limits, routes. 3. **Wallet Service** runs **one transaction**: check sender balance ≥ amount (else 409), `balance -= amount` on sender, `balance += amount` on receiver, insert one ledger row, insert idem_key (unique). **State change:** coins move atomically; if sender & receiver are on different shards, this becomes a 2-phase/saga problem — see §8. 4. Emit a `cheer-event` to **Kafka** *(durable log buffering events so a slow notifier never loses or blocks them)*. 5. **Notifier/Feed** consumes asynchronously → "you got cheered." 6. Return the sender's new balance (read-your-own-write — they see the debit immediately). > 💬 "The money move and the 'notify' are deliberately split: money is synchronous and atomic; the social fan-out is async over Kafka so a viral spike in notifications can't endanger the ledger." ### 6.3 "Real-time view of current balance" This slice: serve balance reads at ~58K QPS in <100ms without hammering Postgres. New piece: a **Redis** read cache, written through on every mutation. | Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | Balance cache | Redis (per-account key) | In-RAM, ~6GB for all DAU, sub-ms reads | Cache/DB coherence to manage | | Read path | Wallet Service read API | Serve cache-first, fall back to DB | — | > 🎙️ **Script (choices):** "Balance reads are the high-volume path, so I front the DB with Redis — an in-memory store. All daily balances fit in about six gigs of RAM, so hit rates are near 100%. The trick for correctness is that the Wallet Service updates Redis *in the same flow* as the committed DB write — write-through — so right after your own cheer you read your new balance from cache. On a cache miss I read the authoritative balance from Postgres and backfill. The DB stays the source of truth; Redis is just a fast lens." ```mermaid flowchart LR C[Client app]:::client -->|1 GET balance| GW[API Gateway]:::svc GW -->|2| WS[Wallet Service]:::svc WS -->|3 read| R[(Redis balance cache)]:::store R -->|4a hit| WS WS -->|4b miss -> read DB| DB[(Ledger + Balance DB
Postgres sharded)]:::store DB -->|5 backfill| R WS -->|6 balance + as_of| C classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe; ``` ↳ reuses existing: API Gateway, Wallet Service, Ledger/Balance DB (§6.1–6.2). 1. Client GETs balance. 2. Gateway authenticates → 3. Wallet Service reads **Redis** first. 4a. Hit → return. 4b. Miss → read Postgres (source of truth). 5. Backfill Redis. 6. Return balance + `as_of`. **Write path keeps Redis fresh:** every committed mutation in §6.1/6.2 does `SET balance` (or atomic `INCRBY`) post-commit, so cache reflects the ledger. > 💬 "Cache-first read, write-through on every committed mutation — so your own actions are always visible to you instantly, and the DB is always the tiebreaker." 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's earn, cheer, and balance end to end. The interesting forks are the atomic cross-shard transfer and the viral hot-account. Want me to go deep there, or somewhere else first?" ### Final (high-level) The reconciled union of all slices: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Clients S[Sender app]:::client C[Client app]:::client end Act[Activity events]:::ext --> GW S -->|cheer| GW[API Gateway
auth + rate limit]:::svc C -->|GET balance| GW GW --> WS[Wallet Service
single money door]:::svc WS -->|atomic debit/credit + ledger
+ idem unique key| DB[(Ledger + Balance DB
Postgres sharded by account_id)]:::store WS -->|cache-first read / write-through| R[(Redis balance cache)]:::store WS -->|emit cheer/earn event| K[[Kafka events]]:::async K --> NF[Notifier / Feed svc]:::svc K --> AN[Analytics / Reconciliation]:::svc AN -.nightly recompute vs ledger.-> DB classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe; ``` Load-bearing columns: **Ledger**(`txn_id, from_acct, to_acct, amount, type, idem_key UNIQUE, status, created_at`), **Balance**(`account_id PK, balance, version`), **Redis**(`bal:{account_id} → balance`). Added **Reconciliation** consumer: nightly re-sums the ledger and compares to balance rows to catch any drift. > 🎙️ **Script (full flow):** "End to end: requests hit the gateway which authenticates and rate-limits, then the Wallet Service — the single door for all money. Writes go atomically to sharded Postgres, the source of truth, and write through to Redis for instant reads. After committing, the service emits an event to Kafka so notifications, feeds, and analytics happen asynchronously without touching the money path. And a reconciliation job continuously re-derives balances from the immutable ledger to prove nothing drifted. The whole philosophy: one atomic ledger write, everything social is async." --- ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Fields (type — note) | Store | Why this store | |---|---|---|---| | **Account/Balance** | `account_id PK (uuid)`, `balance bigint (integer coins, never <0)`, `version bigint (optimistic lock)`, `home_region` | **Postgres**, sharded by `account_id` | Strong consistency + cheap atomic `UPDATE … WHERE balance>=amt`; balance is read/written constantly | | **Ledger (Transaction)** | `txn_id PK`, `from_acct`, `to_acct`, `amount bigint`, `type (earn/cheer)`, `idem_key UNIQUE`, `status (pending/committed/failed)`, `created_at` | **Postgres** (same shard as account), append-only; archived to **S3/Parquet** after 90d | ACID + immutable audit trail; it's the source of truth; cold history → cheap blob | | **Idempotency** | `idem_key PK`, `txn_id`, `result`, `created_at`, TTL 24h | In ledger via **UNIQUE index** (hot) | Dedup in the *same* txn → exactly-once with no extra round trip | | **Balance cache** | `bal:{account_id} → balance` | **Redis**, TTL 1h, write-through | Ephemeral, in-RAM, sub-ms reads; rebuildable from DB so loss is safe | | **Events** | cheer/earn events | **Kafka**, 7-day retention | Durable async buffer; decouples social fan-out from money | **Shard key = `account_id`** so each account's balance + ledger live together → a single-account earn or balance-read is a one-shard transaction. Cross-account cheers may span shards → handled in §8. Per-op consistency: balance write/read **strong** (from primary/cache after own write); notification/feed/analytics **eventual**. > 🎙️ **Script:** "For the ledger and balances I use Postgres because money demands ACID and strong consistency — the debit and credit must commit together, and `balance` carries a not-negative constraint enforced right in the UPDATE. I shard by account id so an account's balance and its history sit on the same shard, making single-account ops a local atomic transaction. The idempotency key is just a unique index inside that same transaction, which gives me exactly-once for free. Redis holds balances only as a fast, rebuildable cache — losing it is harmless because the ledger can always recompute the truth. And Kafka carries the social events because those are fine to be eventually consistent." --- ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the one invariant — *coins are conserved and balances never go negative* — and reason forward from "what makes this atomic and idempotent." ### How do we make a cheer atomic when sender and receiver are on different shards? **#### Bad: update two rows with no transaction (app-level "debit then credit")** **Approach:** Wallet Service does `sender -= 10`, then `receiver += 10` as two separate calls. **Challenges:** ⚠️ **The classic trap** — if the process crashes between the two, coins **vanish** (debited, never credited) or **duplicate** on retry. No atomicity = corrupted money. Disqualifying. **#### Good: single-shard fast path + 2-phase commit for cross-shard** **Approach:** Co-locate via `account_id` so *most* transfers... aren't co-located (random pairs usually differ). For cross-shard, use a coordinator running **2PC** *(two-phase commit: prepare both shards, then commit both — an all-or-nothing handshake)*. **Challenges:** 2PC blocks: if the coordinator dies after "prepare," both rows stay locked (in-doubt) — bad under viral load, and it couples availability of two shards. **#### Great: Saga with a pending-ledger state machine + idempotency (what payments systems actually do)** **Approach:** Model the transfer as a **saga** *(a sequence of local transactions with compensations instead of a global lock)* driven by the ledger's `status`: 1. **Local txn on sender shard:** check funds, `sender -= amt`, write ledger row `status=pending`, idem_key unique. Committed locally. 2. **Local txn on receiver shard:** `receiver += amt`, mark its leg done — keyed by the same `txn_id` (idempotent). 3. **Finalize:** flip ledger `status=committed`. 4. **Compensation:** if step 2 can't complete (receiver invalid/frozen), a recovery worker **refunds** the sender (`sender += amt`, `status=failed`) — idempotently. Each step is a local ACID transaction; the `txn_id`/idem_key makes every step **retry-safe**, so at-least-once delivery becomes effectively exactly-once. ```mermaid flowchart LR WS[Wallet Service]:::svc -->|1 local: debit + pending| SS[(Sender shard
Postgres)]:::store WS -->|2 local: credit by txn_id| RS[(Receiver shard
Postgres)]:::store WS -->|3 mark committed| SS RW[Recovery worker]:::async -.->|4 compensate: refund if step2 fails| SS classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a; ``` **Trade-off:** the receiver's credit is *eventually* (milliseconds, occasionally seconds on retry) consistent — but the **sender's debit and the ledger are immediately consistent and never lost**. That's the right asymmetry: money is never created or destroyed, only briefly in-flight (visible as `pending`). No global lock → survives viral load. > 🎙️ **Script:** "Cross-shard transfers I model as a saga over a pending-ledger state machine instead of a distributed lock. The sender's shard atomically debits and writes a *pending* ledger row; the receiver's shard credits keyed by the same transaction id so it's idempotent; then we mark it committed. If the credit can't land, a recovery worker refunds the sender. Every step is a local ACID transaction and retry-safe, so I get effectively exactly-once without 2PC's locking — coins are never lost, just briefly in-flight." > 🧠 **If they ask "why not just 2PC?":** "2PC gives cleaner semantics but it's a blocking protocol — a coordinator failure leaves rows locked in-doubt, and under viral load that's an availability bomb. The saga trades a brief pending window for non-blocking, crash-safe progress, which is what real payment ledgers use." ### How do we handle a viral account receiving ~10K cheers/sec to one balance row? **#### Bad: everyone does `UPDATE balance += 1 WHERE id = creator`** **Approach:** Direct row update per cheer. **Challenges:** ⚠️ All 10K/s serialize on one row's lock → latency explodes, timeouts cascade, the shard's hot. A single row is the bottleneck; its ceiling is a few hundred contended updates/sec. **#### Good: sharded counters (split the hot balance into N sub-balances)** **Approach:** Split the creator's receive-balance into, say, **100 sub-counters** `bal:{creator}:{0..99}`; each cheer increments a random one. True balance = sum of sub-counters. **Challenges:** writes spread 100x (10K/s → ~100/s each, comfortable), but **reads** must sum 100 keys — fine if reads are rarer than writes for a hot creator (they are). **#### Great: async aggregation via the event log + periodic rollup** **Approach:** For accounts flagged hot, the cheer's **credit leg** isn't a synchronous row update — it's appended to **Kafka**, and a per-creator consumer **batches** increments (e.g. aggregate 5K events → one `+5000` UPDATE every second). Sender debit stays synchronous & atomic (sender must see funds gone instantly); receiver credit becomes a **batched, ordered** apply. Reads serve cache (`approximate + pending delta`), reconciled by the rollup. **Trade-off:** receiver balance is eventually consistent (sub-second to seconds behind), but write amplification on the hot row drops from 10K/s to ~1/s. **This is what real systems do** — collapse many writes into few via the log. ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Many senders]:::client -->|cheers| WS[Wallet Service]:::svc WS -->|debit sync| SS[(Sender shards)]:::store WS -->|credit leg -> log| K[[Kafka per-creator]]:::async K -->|batch e.g. +5000/s| AGG[Aggregator]:::svc AGG -->|one UPDATE/s| RS[(Creator shard)]:::store AGG --> R[(Redis creator balance)]:::store classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2; classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5; classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a; classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1; ``` > 🎙️ **Script:** "A viral creator taking ten thousand cheers a second would melt a single row, so for hot accounts I stop updating the row per cheer. The sender's debit stays synchronous and atomic — they must see their coins leave instantly — but the creator's credit gets appended to a Kafka partition and an aggregator batches thousands of increments into one update per second. That cuts write pressure on the hot row from ten thousand a second to about one, and the creator's balance is just a second or two behind, which nobody notices." > 🧠 **If they ask "how do you know an account is hot?":** "Track per-account write rate; cross a threshold and flip it to aggregated mode dynamically — and flip back when it cools. Sharded counters are the simpler always-on fallback." ### How do we guarantee read-your-own-writes and exactly-once earning? **#### Great: write-through cache + same-transaction idempotency** **Approach:** After a committed mutation, the Wallet Service updates Redis synchronously *before returning* → the actor's next read sees their new balance (read-your-own-writes), even if a replica lags. For exactly-once earn, the **idem_key UNIQUE index lives in the same transaction as the credit** — a replayed earn event throws a unique-violation, we catch it and return the original `txn_id`/balance. No separate dedup store to get out of sync. **Trade-off:** a *different* viewer on a lagging cross-region replica may see a balance a few ms stale — acceptable; only *your own* writes are guaranteed instantly visible. > 🎙️ **Script:** "Read-your-own-writes I get by updating Redis synchronously right after the DB commit and before I return, so your next read can't miss your own action. Exactly-once earning comes from putting the idempotency key as a unique index inside the same transaction as the credit — replay the same event and you hit a unique violation, so I just return the original result instead of crediting twice. There's no separate dedup store to drift." > 🧠 **If they ask about namespace collisions:** "Earn keys are the platform's event ids; cheer keys are client uuids — different prefixes (`earn:`/`cheer:`), so the two can't collide, and the unique index is the final arbiter." ### Single global coordinators? (the treasury) The **treasury account** is the one global hot-spot (every earn debits it). At ~40K earns/sec that's the same hot-row problem → I shard the treasury into **N sub-treasuries** (it can run "negative"/unbounded by design since it's the mint), each earn debits a random one. No single coordinator, no global lock, no sequencer needed — `account_id` sharding means there's **no global counter** in the hot path at all. --- ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost **Availability:** reads **99.99%** (Redis + multi-replica Postgres + multi-AZ); writes **99.9%** (favor refusing over corrupting). **Per-dependency degradation:** - **Redis down** → reads fall back to Postgres primary (slower, still correct); writes unaffected (DB is truth). Degrade, don't fail. - **Kafka down** → money path **unaffected** (it's only post-commit social/analytics); events buffer locally / replay later → notifications delayed, not lost. - **A Postgres shard down** → only accounts on that shard affected; failover to replica (multi-AZ, ~30s). Cross-shard cheers touching it queue as `pending` and complete on recovery (saga). - **Aggregator down (hot path)** → hot-creator credits buffer in Kafka, apply on restart; balance lags but never lost. **RPO/RTO:** Ledger/balance **RPO ≈ 0** (synchronous replica in-region + WAL), **RTO ~1 min** (replica promotion). Cache RPO N/A (rebuildable). **Tested restore:** nightly **reconciliation** re-sums the ledger vs balance rows — this *is* the continuous integrity test; plus quarterly point-in-time restore drills. **Multi-AZ vs multi-region:** synchronous replicas across AZs (RPO 0); cross-region is **async** with each user pinned to a **home region** for writes (avoids multi-master coin conflicts). Region failover = promote the async replica, accepting bounded RPO for that region's users. **Cost (rough monthly):** dominant driver is the **sharded Postgres write tier + storage** (~345TB/yr growing) — order **~$150–250K/mo** at this scale; Redis ~$10K; Kafka ~$20K. The ledger DB, not egress, is the cost center here. Optimization: archive ledger >90d to S3/Parquet (cuts hot storage ~10x). > 🎙️ **Script:** "The failure philosophy is that everything except the atomic ledger write is optional. Redis, Kafka, the aggregator, notifications — any of them can be down and coins are still correct, just slower or delayed. The ledger gets synchronous in-AZ replication for near-zero data loss and one-minute failover, and a reconciliation job continuously re-derives balances from the immutable ledger so I can *prove* nothing drifted. Biggest cost is the Postgres write-and-storage tier, which I tame by archiving old ledger entries to cheap blob storage." --- ## 10. Trade-off Ledger | Decision | Gave up | Reverses when | |---|---|---| | **Saga (pending ledger) over 2PC** for cross-shard | Brief eventual-consistency on receiver credit | If a regulator demanded *synchronously* visible atomic settlement, I'd revisit 2PC/Spanner-style — but accept its locking cost | | **Async aggregation for hot accounts** | Hot-creator balance is seconds-stale | If exact real-time creator balance were a product requirement (e.g. live payout), I'd use sharded counters (real-time sum) despite read cost | | **Single home-region writes** | Cross-region users have higher write latency; no multi-master | If we needed local low-latency writes globally, I'd move to per-region treasuries + conflict-free design, accepting weaker global ordering | These tie back to the opening SLOs: I prioritized **consistency & durability (RPO≈0, never-negative)** over **the last millisecond of write latency** and over **real-time exactness of *other people's* balances** — exactly the asymmetry the requirements implied (money correct > instant for everyone else). > 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'd flag: I chose sagas over two-phase commit, trading a brief pending window for non-blocking crash safety; I chose async aggregation for viral accounts, trading a couple seconds of staleness on their balance for surviving the spike; and I pinned writes to a home region, trading global write latency for avoiding multi-master coin conflicts. All three protect the same thing — coins are never lost or duplicated — at the cost of latency or staleness for non-critical reads." --- ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **1. How do you guarantee a balance never goes negative under concurrency?** The debit is a conditional atomic update: `UPDATE balance SET balance = balance - :amt WHERE account_id = :id AND balance >= :amt`. If zero rows are affected, there weren't enough funds → 409. The row lock serializes concurrent debits on the same account, so two simultaneous cheers can't both pass the check. Optimistic `version` columns are an alternative for lower contention. > 💬 "I make the funds check and the debit one atomic conditional update — if it affects zero rows, you didn't have the coins, so I decline. Concurrency can't sneak a double-spend past a row lock." **2. What exactly happens on a client retry of a cheer?** The client resends with the same Idempotency-Key. The first attempt inserted that key (unique) with the transaction. The retry's insert hits a unique-violation, so we don't apply a second debit — we look up the original `txn_id` and return the same 200 result. So retries are safe and the caller can't tell the difference except they get the original outcome. > 💬 "Same idempotency key means the second request just returns the first result — no second debit, ever." **3. Sender and receiver are on different shards and the process crashes after the debit — where do the coins go?** They sit in the ledger as a `pending` row — debited from the sender, not yet credited. A recovery worker scans for stale `pending` rows and either completes the receiver credit (idempotent by txn_id) or compensates by refunding the sender. Coins are never lost; they're briefly in-flight and the state machine guarantees they resolve to exactly one terminal state. > 💬 "They're not lost — they're in a pending ledger entry, and a recovery worker either finishes the credit or refunds the sender." **4. How do you reconcile / detect corruption?** Because the ledger is the immutable source of truth and balance rows are a derived projection, a nightly job re-sums each account's ledger and compares to its balance row; any mismatch alarms and can auto-heal by recomputing from the ledger. The treasury sum across all accounts must equal coins minted — a global conservation check. > 💬 "Balances are derived, so I can always re-add the ledger and catch any drift — and total coins must equal what the treasury minted." **5. A creator goes viral — walk me through it.** We detect the spike via per-account write rate and flip the creator to aggregated mode: sender debits stay synchronous, but the creator's credits append to a Kafka partition and an aggregator batches thousands into one update per second. Their displayed balance is a second or two behind but never wrong, and the hot row never sees more than ~1 write/sec. > 💬 "I stop updating their row per cheer and batch the credits through Kafka — ten thousand a second becomes one update a second." **6. Why Postgres and not a NoSQL store like Cassandra/DynamoDB?** The core operation is a multi-row atomic transaction with an invariant (non-negative, conserved) — that's exactly what ACID relational engines do well, and exactly what eventually-consistent NoSQL makes painful. NoSQL would force me to rebuild transactions and uniqueness in the app. I shard Postgres for scale, getting horizontal capacity while keeping per-account ACID. If write volume vastly outgrew this, I'd consider Spanner/CockroachDB (distributed SQL) before giving up transactions. > 💬 "Money needs ACID transactions, so I start with relational and shard it, rather than bolt transactions onto NoSQL." **7. How does earn stay exactly-once if the activity service retries?** Each earn carries the platform event id as its idempotency key, stored as a unique index in the same transaction as the credit. A replayed event hits the unique violation and returns the original credit — so the same activity can fire ten times and credit once. > 💬 "The earn's event id is its dedup key inside the same transaction, so replays credit exactly once." **8. Two users cheer each other simultaneously — deadlock?** If transfers lock accounts in transaction order, A→B and B→A could deadlock. I avoid it by always acquiring account locks in a **canonical order** (e.g. sorted by account_id), so both transactions grab locks in the same sequence and one simply waits. Postgres also detects deadlocks and aborts one, which we retry idempotently. > 💬 "I lock accounts in a fixed sorted order so mutual cheers can't deadlock, and retries are safe anyway." **9. How fresh is the balance a user sees? Read-your-own-writes?** Your own writes are guaranteed instantly visible because the Wallet Service updates Redis synchronously after commit and before returning. Another user viewing your balance may see it a few milliseconds stale on a lagging replica, or seconds stale for a viral account in aggregated mode — both acceptable since only the *owner's* view-after-action must be exact. > 💬 "You always see your own latest balance instantly; others might see yours a hair stale, which is fine." **10. How do you stop abuse — spamming cheers or self-cheering to farm coins?** Rate-limit cheers per sender at the gateway, reject self-transfers, and validate earns server-side against the actual activity (never trust client amount). Since the books are conserved (earn = treasury debit), coins can't be conjured; fraud-ML can subscribe to the Kafka event stream to flag patterns asynchronously without slowing the money path. > 💬 "Coins can only move, never be invented, plus rate limits and server-side earn validation — and fraud detection rides the event stream off the hot path." **11. Multi-region: user in EU, what happens?** Each user has a home region where their writes commit; reads serve from the nearest replica. A cheer to a user in another region commits in the sender's home region as a pending ledger entry and replicates; the receiver's credit applies in their home region. We avoid multi-master on the same account, so no coin-conflict resolution is needed. > 💬 "Each user writes in their home region; cross-region cheers settle via the same pending-ledger saga, so we never have two regions fighting over one balance." **12. How would you add 'buy coins with real money' later?** It slots in as another earn-type ledger entry, but now the credit must be tied to a payment-provider transaction id as its idempotency key, and we'd add PCI-compliant payment handling plus stricter audit/reconciliation (real money = regulatory). The ledger abstraction already supports it — it's just a credit from a "purchase" source instead of treasury. > 💬 "Because it's already a ledger, buying coins is just another credit type keyed on the payment id — the hard part added is compliance, not the data model." **13. What's the bottleneck at 10x growth and how do you relieve it?** The Postgres write tier on the busiest shards. Relief: add shards and rebalance by `account_id` (consistent hashing minimizes movement), push more hot accounts into aggregated mode, and offload reads fully to Redis + read replicas. The ledger's append-only nature also lets us partition by time for archival. > 💬 "Write shards bind first; I add shards with consistent hashing and lean harder on aggregation and read replicas." **14. Why is the treasury not a single-point bottleneck?** Every earn debits the treasury, which would be a hot row, so I shard it into many sub-treasuries and debit a random one per earn; the treasury is allowed to be unbounded/negative since it's the mint. There's no global counter or sequencer in the hot path at all — account-id sharding removes the need for one. > 💬 "I split the treasury into many sub-accounts so the mint isn't one hot row — and there's no global counter anywhere in the write path." **15. How do you test that money is never lost in production?** Continuous reconciliation (ledger re-sum vs balance + global conservation check) is the live integrity test, alerting on any drift; we also run chaos drills killing shards mid-saga to verify pending entries resolve, and quarterly point-in-time restore drills to validate RPO/RTO. > 💬 "Continuous reconciliation plus chaos tests on mid-transfer crashes prove coins always resolve to exactly one place." --- > 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "CheerCoin is a wallet, so I treat it as a payments ledger: an immutable, append-only transaction log is the source of truth, and balances are a fast derived projection. Three flows — earn, cheer, view balance — all go through one Wallet Service, the single door for moving money, backed by Postgres sharded by account id for per-account ACID, with Redis for instant write-through balance reads and Kafka for async notifications and analytics off the money path. The two hard problems: cross-shard transfers I solve with a saga over a pending-ledger state machine plus idempotency keys, so coins are never lost or duplicated and there's no blocking 2PC; and viral hot accounts I solve by batching their credits through Kafka, turning ten thousand writes a second on one row into one. Everything except the atomic ledger write can fail without corrupting money, a reconciliation job continuously proves no drift, and where consistency fights latency, I choose consistency — I'd rather decline a cheer than create a coin from nothing."
